title: Turns head side to side
description: Lying on a firm bed or mat, briefly turns their head to each side.
rangeNote: Newborn reflexes vary day to day.

title: Focuses on faces up close
description: Looks at a face held about a hand-span away (8–12 inches / 20–30 cm).
rangeNote: Newborns see best at close range — during feeds is perfect.

title: Calms when comforted
description: Settles when held, rocked, swaddled, or spoken to gently.
rangeNote: Soothing takes practice for both of you.

title: Reacts to loud sounds
description: Startles, blinks, or stills in response to a sudden sound.
rangeNote: A startle (Moro) reflex is normal and healthy.

title: Moves arms and legs actively
description: Kicks and moves both arms and legs when awake and content.

title: Briefly watches a face
description: Holds their gaze on your face for a moment during quiet alert time.

title: Begins a feeding rhythm
description: Starts to settle into more predictable feeding and sleep patterns.
rangeNote: Patterns are still loose this early — feed on demand.

title: Quiets to a familiar voice
description: Calms or grows still when they hear a familiar, soothing voice.

title: Lifts head briefly on tummy
description: Raises their head for a few seconds during tummy time on a firm mat.
rangeNote: Short, frequent tummy time on the floor helps.

title: Follows an object to midline
description: Tracks a slowly moving object with their eyes toward the centre of their body.

title: First social smile
description: Smiles in response to your face or voice — a true social smile.
rangeNote: Often appears around 6–8 weeks; a key milestone in the IAP and TDSC charts.

title: Makes throaty sounds
description: Begins making soft gurgling and cooing sounds beyond crying.

title: Holds head at 45°
description: Lifts and holds their head at about a 45-degree angle during tummy time.

title: Watches faces intently
description: Studies faces and follows moving people or objects with interest.

title: Smiles back at people
description: Smiles responsively when family members smile and talk to them.

title: Coos with vowel sounds
description: Makes 'ooh' and 'aah' cooing sounds, often during back-and-forth play.

title: Pushes up on forearms
description: Lifts head and chest on forearms during tummy time on the floor.
rangeNote: Strength builds week by week with daily floor play.

title: Opens and closes hands
description: Unclenches fists and may swipe at dangling toys.

title: Follows across midline
description: Tracks a moving object smoothly from one side to the other.

title: Enjoys interaction
description: Smiles spontaneously and looks for social play with familiar people.
rangeNote: Some babies laugh around now too.

title: Coos and gurgles
description: Makes a wider range of cooing and gurgling sounds during play.

title: Holds head steady upright
description: Keeps their head steady and upright when held in a sitting position.

title: Brings hands to mouth
description: Discovers their hands and brings them together and to their mouth.

title: Reaches for a dangling toy
description: Swipes at or reaches toward a toy held within reach.

title: Laughs out loud
description: Giggles and laughs during play, peekaboo, and tickles.

title: Takes turns 'talking'
description: Coos and pauses as if waiting for your reply in a back-and-forth.

title: Pushes up on elbows
description: On their tummy, props up on elbows with head lifted and steady.

title: May roll tummy to back
description: Some babies begin rolling from tummy to back.
rangeNote: Rolling commonly starts 4–6 months — give plenty of floor time.

title: Grabs toys
description: Reaches for and grasps a toy, then brings it toward their mouth.

title: Enjoys play with people
description: Smiles, coos, and may fuss when enjoyable play stops.

title: Babbles with expression
description: Babbles and copies some sounds and tones they hear at home.

title: Rolls tummy to back
description: Rolls from their tummy onto their back with more control.

title: Explores with mouth
description: Brings objects to their mouth to investigate them.
rangeNote: Keep small objects well out of reach — mouthing is constant now.

title: Reaches for nearby toys
description: Deliberately reaches for and explores toys with hands and mouth.

title: Recognizes familiar people
description: Lights up for parents and grandparents, and may study new faces.

title: Blows raspberries
description: Makes playful raspberry and squealing sounds.

title: Sits with support
description: Sits propped against a cushion or your body, briefly steady with hands down for balance.
rangeNote: Floor play on a firm mat or dhurrie helps; independent sitting often follows by 6–8 months.

title: Passes objects hand to hand
description: Transfers a toy from one hand to the other.

title: Looks for partly hidden things
description: Searches for a toy that is partly covered by a cloth.

title: Enjoys the mirror
description: Knows familiar faces and enjoys looking at themselves in a mirror.

title: Babbles strings of sounds
description: Strings sounds together like 'ba-ba' or 'ma-ma' (not yet meaning a person).

title: Sits without support
description: Sits steadily on the floor on their own with hands free to play.
rangeNote: Indian and WHO norms place steady sitting anywhere across 6–9 months.

title: Rakes small objects
description: Uses a raking motion to draw small objects toward themselves.

title: Explores by banging
description: Investigates objects by banging, shaking, and mouthing them.

title: Responds to emotions
description: Reacts to others' tone and facial expressions; may be wary of strangers.

title: Responds to their name
description: Turns toward you when you call their name.

title: Rocks on hands and knees
description: Gets onto hands and knees and rocks, or scoots to move.

title: Picks up with finger and thumb
description: Begins using an emerging pincer grasp for small bits of soft food or toys.

title: Looks for hidden objects
description: Searches for a fully hidden toy — a sign of object permanence.

title: Prefers caregivers
description: Shows a clear preference for familiar people; stranger wariness is common.
rangeNote: Separation anxiety is a normal, healthy sign of attachment.

title: Babbles 'mamama', 'dadada'
description: Repeats consonant–vowel chains in long babbling strings.

title: Crawls or scoots
description: Moves across the floor by crawling, scooting, or rolling.
rangeNote: Crawling styles and timing vary widely; some babies skip crawling altogether.

title: Uses a pincer grasp
description: Picks up small objects neatly between thumb and forefinger.

title: Plays peekaboo
description: Enjoys peekaboo and watches where a dropped object goes.

title: Has favorite people and toys
description: Shows clear preferences and may be clingy with favorites.

title: Understands 'no'
description: Pauses or reacts when they hear 'no' or 'nahi'; copies sounds and gestures.

title: Pulls to stand
description: Pulls up to standing while holding onto furniture or your hands.

title: Bangs two objects together
description: Holds an object in each hand and bangs them together.

title: Explores how things work
description: Pokes, drops, and turns objects to see what happens.

title: Plays interactive games
description: Enjoys back-and-forth games and may begin waving 'ta-ta' / bye-bye.

title: Babbles like conversation
description: Babbles with the rhythm and tone of real speech in your home language.

title: Cruises along furniture
description: Steps sideways while holding onto furniture for support.

title: Puts objects in a container
description: Drops or places objects into a cup, katori, or container.

title: Imitates gestures
description: Copies simple gestures like clapping, namaste, or waving.

title: Repeats actions for attention
description: Repeats sounds or actions that get a reaction from you.

title: Uses simple gestures
description: Shakes head for 'no' or waves; may say one clear word.

title: Stands alone, may step
description: Stands without support briefly and may take first steps.
rangeNote: Independent walking ranges widely, often 12–15 months — later is still normal.

title: Neat pincer grasp
description: Picks up tiny objects precisely with thumb and fingertip.

title: Follows simple directions
description: Follows a simple request, especially with a gesture (e.g. 'give me', 'do').

title: Plays social games
description: Claps, hands you a book, does namaste, and copies you during play.

title: Says first words
description: Says one or more words like 'mama', 'papa', 'dada', or 'nahi' with meaning.
